context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Initiatives, Pathways and Stressors

Recommended due date: July 25 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will build on your Week 6 research by analyzing how your city's climate initiatives connect to transformation pathways and address environmental stressors.

Assignment Task
Complete the Initiatives, Pathways and Stressors Table using your Week 6 findings.

Step 1: List Key Initiatives
Identify the key initiatives and actions being proposed and implemented in your case study city (from your Week 6 research).

Step 2: Connect to Pathways
For each initiative, identify which of the 6 Pathways for Urban Transformation it relates to.

Step 3: Link to Stressors
Identify which environmental stressors each initiative addresses.

Resources
See the example provided in the course materials of a completed Initiatives, Pathways and Stressors Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I’m stuck on the city case study table rn 😅 can u help me figure out how to match my city’s initiatives to the 6 pathways and the environmental stressors?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, happy to help you work through this! Before we dive in — which city are you working on, and what initiatives did you identify in your Week 6 research? Share what you have so far and we can go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for general guidance on matching initiatives to pathways and stressors. Rather than explaining the framework to them, I should first find out what they already know and what they've done so far. This will help me assess their starting point and guide them through discovery rather than giving them a lecture. I'll ask them to share their work so far.

exchanges.student: I’m using Jakarta, and rn I’ve got flood control, public transit expansion, and mangrove/coastal restoration. I think those fit somehow, but idk which pathway labels match best.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — those three initiatives have clear relevance to Jakarta's climate situation. Before we try to match them: can you list the 6 Pathways for Urban Transformation from your course materials? That's the right place to start.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good initiatives identified and is now asking which pathway labels match. Rather than telling them, I should prompt them to recall or reference the 6 pathways from the course materials. This is a guided discovery move — they have the resources available and should engage with them first before I scaffold further.

exchanges.student: ngl I don’t remember all 6 exactly rn 😭 can u give me a quick hint or the first words so I can jog my memory?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but that's something you should look up in your course materials — the 6 pathways are defined there and the assignment references them directly. Take a quick look and come back with the list. It'll be worth it to have them in front of you as we work through the table!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the pathway names, which would essentially be doing part of the assignment for them. I should redirect them to their course materials instead — this is a reasonable expectation for a college student. I won't provide the pathway names, but I'll encourage them to look it up, which is a good study habit anyway.

exchanges.student: ok fair, I found them I think. Can I paste the list here and then try matching Jakarta’s initiatives to them?
exchanges.tutor: Yes! Go ahead and paste the list, and while you're at it, take a first shot at matching each of your three initiatives to a pathway. Even a rough guess is fine — just show me your reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing initiative and willingness to engage with the material. I should encourage them to share the list and their own matching attempts together, rather than just the list — this keeps them in the driver's seat and avoids me doing the matching for them.

exchanges.student: Yep — I found them. My rough match is: flood control → adaptation/resilience path, public transit expansion → mitigation/low-carbon mobility, and mangrove restoration → nature-based resilience maybe? I’m not totally sure if I’m naming them the same way as the course does.
exchanges.tutor: Your intuition seems reasonable! But 'maybe' and 'I'm not sure' are flags — how do your labels compare to the exact pathway names in the course materials? Check the wording carefully and see if your matches hold up.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuine attempt at matching and is showing good intuition. Rather than validating or correcting their specific pathway labels, I should prompt them to cross-check their own language against the exact pathway names from the course materials. This keeps them engaged with the source material and avoids me doing the verification for them.

exchanges.student: yeah that’s where I’m stuck tbh — my ideas make sense, but the exact course wording is messing me up. Can u help me check if I’m mapping by concept the right way first?
exchanges.tutor: I think you're overthinking this a bit. The concepts you described ARE the right direction — now it's just about committing to the exact course language. Pick one of your three initiatives, find the closest matching pathway name word-for-word from your materials, and paste both here. Let's work from something concrete.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles a bit — they have the materials, they have their intuitions, but they're asking me to validate their conceptual mapping without committing to the exact course language. This is a pattern I should gently push back on. The exact wording matters for the assignment, and I should encourage them to commit to specific pathway names from the materials rather than working around them.

exchanges.student: ok, for public transit expansion I think the closest pathway is the one about sustainable transport/mobility. Does that sound right, or am I mixing initiative vs pathway?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track conceptually, but 'sustainable transport/mobility' — is that the exact pathway name from your course materials, or are you paraphrasing? Precision matters here. And once you nail that, what environmental stressor do you think public transit expansion addresses in Jakarta's context?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making progress but still looking for validation rather than committing to exact course language. I should push them to be more precise and use the actual pathway name from the course, while also nudging them toward the next step — linking to stressors — so we don't dwell too long on pathway matching alone.

exchanges.student: I’m probably paraphrasing tbh. For stressor, maybe air pollution and heat? but idk if “traffic congestion” counts since it says environmental stressors.
exchanges.tutor: Good question — what makes something an *environmental* stressor versus, say, a social or infrastructural one? Think about how the course defines the term, and that should answer your traffic congestion question. Also, don't forget to go back and nail down that exact pathway name first!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress — their stressor thinking is solid and they're asking a genuinely good conceptual question about whether traffic congestion counts as an environmental stressor. I should help them think through this distinction themselves rather than answering it for them. I'll also gently remind them to go back and get the exact pathway name before moving on.

exchanges.student: ok so traffic congestion prob isn’t environmental by itself. I think air pollution def is, and maybe emissions/urban heat too?
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning — you're distinguishing well between types of stressors. Now apply the same process to flood control and mangrove restoration: for each one, what pathway (exact name from your materials) and what environmental stressors? Take a shot at both.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning well about stressors and arriving at solid answers on their own. This is a good moment to affirm their direction briefly and then push them to consolidate — they've now worked through one initiative reasonably well. I should nudge them to apply the same process to the other two initiatives to keep momentum and avoid dwelling further on this one.

exchanges.student: I think flood control links to an adaptation/resilience-type pathway and addresses flooding, heavy rainfall, and sea-level rise. Mangrove restoration prob fits a nature/ecosystem pathway and addresses coastal erosion, storm surge, and sea-level rise.
exchanges.tutor: You've developed solid reasoning across all three initiatives — good work. The one thing still missing is the exact pathway names from your course materials (you've been paraphrasing throughout). Go back, confirm the precise wording, and then write up your completed table. That's the deliverable — time to put it all together!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through all three initiatives and is showing solid conceptual understanding. We're at exchange 10 and they've made good progress. This is a good moment to push them to consolidate everything into their actual table rather than continuing to discuss. I should acknowledge their progress, note the remaining gap (exact pathway names), and ask them to write up their table.
